Tafseer:
The meaning of 'Al-Haqqah':
'Al-Haqqah' means that which is bound to happen, the occurrence of which is intellectually and morally necessary, and which is completely inevitable and definitive. This single word stands in place of a complete sentence. The opinion of those who regarded 'ma-l-Haqqah' (What is the Inevitable Reality) as its predicate (khabar) is incorrect. This style of expression is adopted when the objective is to startle the listener, especially a heedless listener. In such a scenario, mentioning only the subject (mubtada) is sufficient, and there is no need for a predicate, so that the entire attention of the listener is focused on the subject. The ambiguity thus created in the sentence serves to attract the listener's attention.
'Al-Haqqah' is among the names of the Resurrection. This name manifests both its inevitability and reality, as well as its intellectual and moral necessity. The details of the proofs of both these aspects have appeared in the previous Surahs, some indications are also present in this Surah, and highly important aspects will be clarified in the subsequent Surahs as well.
Primarily, it refers to the Resurrection itself, but implicitly it also includes the punishment that inevitably befell a nation when it rejected its messenger. This is because that punishment is both an introduction to the Resurrection and a confirmation of it, and it actually arrives as a retribution for denying the Resurrection. Allah's messengers warned of two punishments simultaneously: the punishment of the Resurrection and the punishment that is the inevitable consequence of denying the Resurrection. When nations denied the Resurrection and demanded the punishment—which was threatened as a consequence of denial—as a test of the messenger's truthfulness, then after the proof was established, this punishment befell them. Since every word of Allah Almighty and every warning of His messengers is true, this punishment also holds the status of 'Haqqah'—meaning it is bound to happen.

'What is the Inevitable Reality?' (ma-l-Haqqah) - This question is to express its terror, its horror, and its irresistible nature, which is further explained by the subsequent words: 'And what can make you know what is the Inevitable Reality?' (wa ma adraka ma-l-Haqqah), meaning: who can know and who can describe what this inevitable event is, and what will befall those who are mocking it with such audacity today when it manifests!
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 2 and 3 was combined in the original text.]

The meaning of 'Al-Qari'ah':
And regarding the inevitable event of which they were warned, the history of the messengers and their nations is presented as testimony—showing that just as the Quraysh are denying the punishment and the Resurrection, Thamud and Aad also denied them, the end of which came before them. Here, the word 'Al-Qari'ah' is used to describe the punishment and the Resurrection, which means 'the striking one' or 'the knocking one'. In the Qur'an, this characteristic of both the divine punishment and the Resurrection is explained: no one knows the time of their arrival. They will appear suddenly, and just as someone suddenly knocks at the door and startles those sleeping untroubled, these too will cause a major upheaval.

The meaning of 'Taghiyah':
It is stated: as for Thamud, they were destroyed by 'At-Taghiyah'. 'Taghiyah' refers to something that exceeds its limits and bounds. In this Surah, the rain that drowned the nation of Noah is described as: 'the water overflowed [exceeded its limits]' (tagha-l-ma'u). Allah Almighty punishes nations in accordance with their behavior; when a nation adopts the way of rebellion (tughyan), Allah Almighty unleashes one of His creations—which are otherwise subservient for human benefit—releasing it from its bounds so that it becomes 'At-Taghiyah' and destroys them. Thamud also, as is clear from: (Thamud denied [their messenger] by reason of their transgression) (Ash-Shams, 91:11), fell into rebellion against their Lord, so Allah Almighty unleashed a calamity (taghiyah) upon them.
What was this calamity? There is no explanation here, but the indications in various places of the Qur'an have been explained under Surah Adh-Dhariyat. It appears from them that the destruction of Thamud was through a thunderbolt (sa'iqah) that appeared from within the striped clouds of winter. Although winter clouds and thunder are not unusual under normal circumstances, Allah Almighty can make them a resurrection for nations whenever He wills.
In this age, science has progressed greatly, and human beings have apparently succeeded in subserviating many forces of nature; yet, even today we see that whenever Allah wills, He exposes the helplessness of all science and scientists.

The meaning of 'Atiyah':
This refers to the end of Aad—showing that a violent, freezing wind (sar-sar) was unleashed upon them, which devastated them completely. Just as the thunderbolt was described as 'taghiyah' in the account of Thamud, the wind here is described by the attribute 'atiyah', which means a wind that became rebellious and uncontrollable. Allah Almighty made the wind subservient for human benefit, and it is indispensable for human life and survival; but when human beings fall into rebelliousness, Allah Almighty can relax its reins and turn this subservient wind into a punishment whenever He wills.

Depiction of the wind turned into a punishment:
This portrays the wind turned into a punishment—showing that the wind Allah made subservient for human benefit was unleashed upon Aad as a punishment, blowing over them for seven nights and eight days to uproot them completely. 'Hasm' and 'husum' mean to eradicate completely.
'And you would see the people therein fallen as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees' (fa-tara-l-qawma fiha sar'a ka-annahum a'jazu nakhlin khawiyah) - The address in 'tara' (you see) is general on such occasions, and 'Al-Qawm' (the people) carries the meaning of opponents and rivals here. Aad, due to their rebelliousness ('utuww), had risen as if they were rivals to God; therefore, the use of this word here is highly appropriate.
The meaning is: whoever among you looked at them would see that Allah's punishment had laid them prostrate in the field, as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees rolling here and there by the force of the wind.
The reference of the pronoun 'fiha' (therein) can be either the wind or the land of Aad. According to the rules of Arabic, both are correct, and both meanings fit here.

The irresistible nature of divine punishment:
This is linked to 'And you would see' (fa-tara) mentioned above. It is as if the settlements of Aad are brought before the listener's eye of imagination to ask: look far and wide; do you see any soul surviving from the entire nation? The meaning is: when Allah's punishment befalls a nation, it devastates them in this manner! Foolish are those who demand to see it. This is not something to be demanded, but something from which refuge should be sought.

Reference to Pharaoh and the nation of Lot:
'Al-Mu'tafikah' means 'the overturned ones'. Here, it refers to the settlements of the nation of Lot. They were overturned by an earthquake, and 'hasib'—the wind carrying stones—covered them with sand and pebbles. Above, two of the ancient nations were mentioned; now, reference is made to the settlements of Pharaoh and the nation of Lot, which the Quraysh frequently passed by. It is stated: they too committed the same crime that Aad and Thamud committed, and they faced the same end.

This points to the nature of their crime—showing that they disobeyed the messenger of their Lord, so Allah seized them with a seizure from which they could not escape.
Disobeying the messenger is rebellion against God: The words 'And they disobeyed the messenger of their Lord' (wa 'asaw rasula rabbihim) clarify the gravity of their crime, as the messenger of God is the ambassador of the King of the universe. Therefore, those who disobey him raise the flag of rebellion against the King of the universe, deserving the punishment of rebels.
A decisive seizure: 'a seizure exceeding in severity' (akhdhatan-rabiyah) refers to a seizure that cannot be resisted and is beyond human endurance. One type of seizure by Allah Almighty is intended merely for warning and reminding, from which a person can escape; but when a nation dares to raise the flag of rebellion against God, He seizes them with a seizure that is impossible to endure.

Reference to the nation of Noah:
Finally, reference is also made to the incident of the nation of Noah, which had occurred even before the aforementioned incidents. Thus, the entire history of the messengers is brought briefly before the listener.
The style of mentioning this incident is one of bestowing favor upon the addressees (the Quraysh). They are reminded: we, out of Our grace, gave shelter to your ancestors in Noah's ark. They deserved this shelter because they believed in Allah's messenger—Noah (peace be upon him). If they had not believed, they too would have been drowned along with their entire nation. Since you are their descendants, this favor reaches you indirectly. Today, you should not forget this history of yours. If you forget it and adopt the way of disobedience instead of following the Messenger, there is no reason why God should not treat you in the same manner He treated those who disobeyed Noah.

The purpose of narrating past accounts:
The reference of the pronoun 'laj'alaha' (that We might make it) is not merely the ark ('jariyah'), but this entire history of Allah's mercy and wrath. Examples of using a pronoun in this manner exist in this book. The meaning is: We showed this manifestation of Our mercy and wrath to the survivors of Noah's flood so that they would preserve it, learn a lesson from it, and pass it down from generation to generation. But unfortunately, you forgot it, and today you have risen to oppose your Messenger just as the nation of Noah rose.

Worldly punishments are testimonies to the punishment of the Hereafter:
After pointing to the historical incidents of punishment, this points to the occurrence of the Resurrection—showing that just as We did not have to make any special preparation to bring punishment upon nations, but brought it in the twinkling of an eye whenever We willed, similarly We do not have to make any preparation to bring the Resurrection. Rather, a single blast will be blown into the Trumpet, which will cause the upheaval of the Resurrection.

'And the earth and the mountains are lifted and leveled with a single blow' (wa humilati-l-ardu wa-l-jibalu fa-dukkata dakkataw-wahidah) - And do not regard this earth and its mountains as things that would require any effort for Us to devastate; rather, We will lift both of them together—the earth in one hand and its mountains in the other—and smash them against each other in a single blow, reducing them to dust. It is as if they were two glass cups smashed against each other and shattered instantly. Recall here that when the deniers of the Resurrection mocked it, they would ask: 'When the Resurrection comes, will it also smash these mountains?' implying that this is impossible, and therefore, in their view, the Resurrection is merely a fantasy.

'Then on that Day, the Occurrence will occur' (fa-yawma'idhin waqa'ati-l-waqi'ah) - It is stated: on that Day, that which is destined to occur will occur, which you regard as highly unlikely.
In the preceding verses, the Resurrection was described by words like 'Haqqah' and 'Qari'ah'; here, it is described by the word 'Waqi'ah', which points to the reality that if anyone regards it as unlikely, let them do so—with Allah Almighty, it is a solid reality that is bound to happen one day.

The state of the heaven on the Day of Resurrection:
After describing the fate of the earth and its mountains, this states the condition of the heaven: on that Day, it will also split open. 'And it is on that Day frail' (wa hiya yawma'idhin wahiyah) - That is, while today it appears solid and firm to those who look at it, and not the slightest sign of a defect or rift can be found in it despite best efforts, on that Day it will become completely frail and weak, dispersing like wool and smoke.

The state of the angels on the Day of Resurrection:
This points to the condition of the angels after the splitting of the heaven—showing that they will be gathered at its edges and boundaries. The meaning is: they too will be struck with panic by this upheaval. This is stated to warn those polytheists who rely on the angels, assuming they will protect them and intercede for them.
'And the Throne of your Lord will be carried above them on that Day by eight [angels]' (wa yahmilu 'arsha rabbika fawqahum yawma'idhin thamaniyah) - That is, due to this upheaval, the entire administrative staff will be relieved of their duties and step aside, leaving only the bearers of the divine Throne, which will be carried by eight angels.
The conditions of the Resurrection described in the Holy Qur'an belong to the category of 'mutashabihat' (allegorical/unclear verses). To bring them closer to our comprehension, they are described in words that establish a basic concept in our minds. These conditions belong to an unseen world; to give a concept of them, this is the only possible method. Knowing their actual reality is not possible for us in this world. Therefore, the Qur'an directs us to maintain a general belief in them exactly as they are described, without prying into their actual reality, lest one falls into fitnah.

Details of the recompense:
It is stated: on that Day, you will all be presented, and not a secret of yours will remain hidden. 'Being presented' obviously refers to being presented before Allah. On that Day, the entire sheet of the heavens and earth will be folded up; therefore, there will be no place to hide and nothing to conceal.

Following the presentation before Allah's court, this states the details of the recompense that everyone must face. First, the state of the believers is described: whoever is given his record in his right hand will jump with joy upon seeing it, saying to others: 'Here, read my record!'
The particle 'ha' is merely a sound (like 'O' or 'Ah'), but it is used on occasions when one wants to say: 'Here, take it.' Here, the plural marker 'um' is placed between 'ha' and 'iqra'u' merely to fill the gap between the two. Examples of such additions have appeared previously. In 'kitabiyah' (my record), the 'ha' is the 'ha' of pause (ha-us-sakt), which is added merely to maintain the rhyme. Examples of this have appeared previously and are coming further on.

Alongside this, he will also state the cause of his great success: 'I always maintained the thought in my heart that I would face my record of life one day.' The meaning is: this very thought protected me, and I succeeded in obtaining a record that I can present before others with great joy.
An overwhelming thought is sufficient to believe in the recompense: 'Dhann' here is used in the sense of an overwhelming thought (dhann-i-ghalib). The signs in the universe, within ourselves, and the teachings of the prophets and wise men provide such solid proofs of the recompense that unless a person is completely dense and careless, his heart bears witness that this life will not end without consequence; rather, it is inevitable to face the account one day. Although he may not possess the absolute certainty that comes from seeing something with one's eyes, he possesses an overwhelming thought that prevents him from taking the risk of ignoring the Hereafter. The faith in the Hereafter generated by this overwhelming thought strengthens gradually through spiritual experiences, until it attains the level of absolute certainty. If a person ignores this overwhelming thought and waits to believe only when he attains absolute certainty, this wait will end only on the Day when he sees everything with his eyes, and faith on that Day will be of no benefit.
It is stated: these people will be in a pleasant life. They will have whatever they desire, residing in high gardens whose fruits and clusters will be hanging close over their heads.

High gardens and close clusters: Note the contrast between 'high' (aliyah) and 'close' (daniyah)—showing that while the gardens will be high, their fruits and clusters, which are the primary objective, will be very close. The Arabs, whose gardens contained rows of palm trees along the boundaries, pomegranate trees in the middle, and grapevines, had no difficulty in understanding this contrast. Indeed, the prominent beauty of a garden is that it is located on a high ground while its clusters hang low and within reach.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 22 and 23 was combined in the original text.]

That is, admitting them into such gardens, they will be told: 'Eat and drink with satisfaction. This eating and drinking will be wholesome and highly beneficial for you.' The blessings of this world can become a source of misery if moderation is not observed or if proper gratitude is not offered, but there is no such fear regarding these blessings. The linguistic analysis and grammatical position of the word 'hani'an' were clarified in the commentary of Surah At-Tur.
'for what you sent before you in the days past' (bima aslaftum fi-l-ayyami-l-khaliyah) - That is: 'This which you have received is the recompense for the deeds you performed in the world. Here, you do not have to perform any labor. You are fully entitled to it, and this is an eternal blessing for you. It will increase constantly, and there is no fear of any decrease. You have already completed the labor required for it; now, simply enjoy it.'

The state of the people of the left hand: This describes the condition of those whose records will be given in their left hands. They will beat their heads upon seeing it, saying: 'O, would that our record had not been given to us, and we had not known what our account is! O, would that the death we died in the world had been final!' The reference of the pronoun 'laha' (it) is death. When there is context, using a pronoun in this manner without a prior explicit noun is acceptable; examples of this have appeared previously.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 25 and 26 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 25, 26, and 27 was combined in the original text.]

That is, they will say with deep regret: 'The wealth we accumulated with such care and kept counting, of what use has it been!' The particle 'ma' here can be negative, but from the perspective of expressing regret, taking it as interrogative is more appropriate in my view.

'Gone from me is my authority' (halaka 'anni sultaniyah) - The preposition 'anni' after 'halaka' indicates that 'halaka' implies 'gone' (dhahaba) or 'become distant' (ba'uda). 'Sultan' means power and authority—showing that they will say with deep regret: 'The power of which we boasted, and the pride of which blinded us to this Day, has also been stripped away.'

That is, while they are wailing in this manner, the command will be issued: 'Seize him, shackle him, then burn him in Hell, then bind him in a chain whose length is seventy cubits!' Some places of the Qur'an indicate that the wealthy capitalists who accumulated wealth and kept counting it will be cast into Hell and bound to columns with heavy chains, so that they may taste the heat of the wealth upon which they sat like dragons. The details of this will appear in Surah Al-Humazah, if Allah wills.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 30 and 31 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 30, 31, and 32 was combined in the original text.]

The indictment:
This states the crime for which he deserved this wrath and punishment: 'He did not believe in the Great Allah.' This points to the reality that his attitude—worshipping wealth and lamenting today: 'Of what use has my wealth been!'—testifies that he had no faith in God. If he had believed in the Great Allah, he would have feared His majesty, knowing that he had to present himself before Him one day to account for the wealth He bestowed, and that He is such a Great Being that no one can escape His grasp.

'And did not feel the urge to feed the needy' (wa la yahuddu 'ala ta'ami-l-miskeen) - That is: he neither spent on the needy himself nor urged others to do so. Those who are miserly not only avoid spending in the way of Allah, but also wish that others do not spend either, so that their own miserliness remains hidden.
In Surah Al-Ma'un, this same point is expressed as:
(For that is the one who drives away the orphan, and does not feel the urge to feed the needy) (2-3).
In the verse under discussion, the reality is clarified: those who possess wealth but drive away the poor and needy do not actually believe in the Great Allah, even if they claim faith with great volume. Similarly, in Surah Al-Ma'un, the prayers of those whose miserliness is such that they withhold even small, everyday items are declared completely hollow. He states:
(So woe to those who pray, [but] who are heedless of their prayer, who make show [of their deeds] and withhold [simple] assistance) (4-7).
Pondering over both of these places, the reality is clear: a person who possesses wealth but does not help orphans and the needy is neither credible in his faith nor is his prayer of any weight, even if he claims faith and displays his prayers.

That is, the punishment for his miserliness is that on this Day, he has no friend or helper here. Who would sympathize on the Day of Judgment with the one who recognized neither the right of God nor that of His creation? It should be noted that those who are wealthy but miserly find no sympathy even in this world; so who would rise to sympathize with them in the world of recompense?

'Ghisleen' refers to the discharge and washings of wounds. Since they regarded their wealth solely as a means for their own self-indulgence and pleasure, and by withholding the rights of the poor, they turned their wealth into an impurity, on the Day of Resurrection they will have nothing to eat or drink but this discharge of wounds. Note that a person's wealth is purified by spending in the cause of Allah. If a person does not spend, his entire wealth becomes a heap of impurity, the reality of which will appear before him in the Hereafter in this form.

That is: 'None will eat it except the sinners.' The reason is probably that since their crime is of a specific nature, their food will also be specific. The similarity between the crime and the punishment has been discussed in various places in this book.

The use of 'la' before an oath: The particle 'la' appearing before an oath has been discussed in its relevant place—showing that it is neither redundant nor does it negate the oath; rather, it is used to refute the listener's assumption before swearing the oath to prove the contrary. Just as in common speech we say: 'No, by God, this is not the case, but this is the case,' similarly, the discourse here begins with 'No', showing that the listener's assumption is so wrong that the speaker is not willing to delay its refutation even for a moment.
Refutation of a baseless accusation of the deniers: We have clarified regarding oaths in the Qur'an that they are generally sworn as testimony and proof of the claim. Here, the primary claim, which serves as the core theme of the Surah, is the proof of the recompense. The deniers were rejecting this claim, and to do so, instead of answering the arguments from nature, human soul, intellect, and scriptures presented by the Qur'an, they tried to target the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) with the accusation that he was a poet or a soothsayer, asserting that just as devils and jinn cast words upon soothsayers and poets, similarly some jinn or devil casts this speech upon him, which he presents with the claim that an angel brings it to him from Allah Almighty. Here, this baseless accusation of theirs is refuted, and for this, an oath is sworn by both the visible world and the unseen world.
The testimony of the visible and unseen worlds to the Resurrection: The proofs of the Resurrection and recompense presented by the Qur'an have appeared in the previous Surahs and are discussed in this Surah as well. Pondering over them, you will realize that they relate to the signs in the universe and within ourselves that can be seen with the eyes, as well as to the attributes of Allah and the conditions of the Hereafter that cannot be seen with the eyes but can be understood by intellect. In this Surah, the proof of recompense is established first from the history of past nations and the ruins of their destruction, and then the conditions of the unseen world are described—which the people of the right hand and the people of the left hand must face. Obviously, one of these relates to the world whose testimony is present in the pages of history and the ruins of the earth, while the other relates to the unseen world which, although it cannot be seen with the eyes here, is accepted by intellect because the Creator's attributes and the incidents of retribution in this world bear witness to it. Swearing by both of these categories of proofs, the deniers of recompense are informed that the Resurrection the Qur'an is warning of is a solid reality. The proofs of both the visible and unseen worlds support it. Do not try to reject it by calling it the speech of a poet or a soothsayer. This is not the casting of a devil or a jinn, but a revelation brought by a noble messenger.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 38 and 39 was combined in the original text.]

The attributes of the bearer of the Qur'an: 'Indeed, it is the word of a noble messenger' (innahu la-qawlu rasulin kareem) refers to Gabriel. The use of the attribute 'kareem' (noble) is intended to refute the opponents' assumption mentioned above. It is stated: the one who brings this speech is not a jinn or a devil, as you claim, but a noble messenger of Allah. In a similar context, this same point is stated in Surah At-Takwir, which supports this view:
(Indeed, it is the word of a noble messenger, possessed of power and with the Owner of the Throne, secure [in position], obeyed there [in the heavens] and trustworthy.) (At-Takwir, 81:19-21).
In Surah Ash-Shu'ara, this accusation of the deniers is also thoroughly refuted; refer to it for details.

This states the same point in a negative style: it is neither the word of a poet nor of a soothsayer. If you were willing to believe and appreciate the reminder, it would have become clear to you on its own that this cannot be the speech of a poet or a soothsayer. But the trouble is: the desire to believe and the effort to understand are very little in you, and those who lack this effort find excuses to flee.
Why the Qur'an cannot be considered the word of a poet or a soothsayer: This was answered in detail in Surah Ash-Shu'ara; refer to it for details.
Depicting the inner state of the deniers: In 'little is that you believe' (qaleelan ma tu'minun) and 'little is that you remember' (qaleelan ma tadhakkarun), both verbs carry the meaning of the 'intention' of the action. This points to the actual cause of their aversion: if you possessed the intention to believe or the desire to benefit from the reminder, you would have easily distinguished between precious gems and worthless stones; but this intention is rarely generated in you.
This perfectly depicts the inner state of the deniers. After all, the leaders of the Quraysh were not so devoid of taste or foolish as to not understand the difference between the speech of Allah and the words of their poets and soothsayers. They understood the difference, and occasionally the desire to confess the truth must have arisen in them. However, under the weight of their desires, this desire was so suppressed that it rarely arose, and even if it did, it was too weak to bring about any effective change. Only a fortunate few succeeded in emerging from the veils of their desires. Note that this describes the state of the leaders of the Quraysh, not their common people. The character of their leadership was also portrayed in the previous Surah.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 41 and 42 was combined in the original text.]

The actual source of the Qur'an:
This states the same point in a positive manner: it is a revelation from the Lord of the worlds. The analysis of the word 'tanzil' (revelation) was explained in its relevant place—showing that it contains the meanings of care and gradual sending. The arrangement Allah Almighty made to protect it from the reach of the devils is mentioned in various places in the Qur'an, and we have explained it; it will be further detailed in Surah Al-Jinn, if Allah wills.
'from the Lord of the worlds' (mir-rabbi-l-'alameen) manifests both its majesty and the disastrous end of denying it. The meaning is: this is a speech revealed by the King of the universe. If you do not appreciate it, we can only express regret over your deprivation and disastrous end.

Response to the accusation of the disbelievers:
This is the response to the accusation of the disbelievers who claimed regarding the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'He fabricates all of this on his own but asserts to us that this speech is revealed to him from Allah Almighty.' It is stated: the one whom Allah appoints as His messenger is His ambassador and the bearer of the great trust of revelation; therefore, his supervision is extremely strict. He cannot dare to make any alteration in it on his own. If he were to attribute even a single word falsely to Us, We would seize him by our powerful hand and cut his main artery, and then no one could save him from Us.
This is stated in a style that clarifies the strict supervision to which a messenger is subjected by Allah Almighty due to his official responsibilities, and also answers the demand of the disbelievers who urged the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'If we are to believe in this Qur'an, either bring another Qur'an besides this or alter it to make it acceptable to us.' In Surah Yunus, their demand and its response are recorded as:
(And when Our distinct verses are recited to them, those who do not expect the meeting with Us say, 'Bring us a Qur'an other than this or change it.' Say, 'It is not for me to change it on my own accord. I only follow what is revealed to me. Indeed, I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous Day.') (Yunus, 10:15).

In 'We would have seized him by the right hand' (la-akhadhna minhu bil-yameen), commentators have generally translated it as 'We would have seized his right hand', but I do not find this translation correct. According to the rules of Arabic, it should be translated as: 'We would have seized him by Our powerful hand'. I have adopted this translation, and upon referring to the commentary of Ibn Jarir, I found it supported there as well.

'Then We would have cut from him the life-vein' (thumma la-qata'na minhu-l-wateen) - 'Wateen' means the main artery, the life-vein, or the vein of the heart. The meaning is: We are not distant from him; indeed, his life-vein is in Our grasp, and We could sever it instantly, ending his life.

'And there is no one of you who could prevent [Us] from him' (fa-ma minkum-min ahadin 'anhu hajizeen) - This addresses the Quraysh: you are demanding alterations in the Qur'an according to your wishes, but who among you could save him from the grasp of God! Since the word 'ahad' (anyone) can carry a plural meaning—as in: (you are not like anyone of the women) (Al-Ahzab, 33:32)—the use of the plural 'hajizeen' (preventers) is in perfect accordance with Arabic grammar.
Messengers are protected in accordance with their responsibility: It should be noted that Allah Almighty exercises such strict supervision over those whom He appoints to the position of messengership, because the treasure of revelation is in their custody and its protection is necessary. This does not mean that every person who fabricates a lie against God will have his neck broken immediately. Fabricating lies aside, there are many who insult God, yet they are granted respite in this world; they will face their end in the Hereafter. However, a true messenger of God can neither fabricate a lie against God nor alter His message under anyone's pressure. The protection (ismah) granted to messengers is intended to preserve God's Shariah. Their minor oversight or error could become a fitnah for the entire creation; therefore, if they commit even a minor lapse, Allah Almighty corrects it immediately. Others are not granted this level of protection because they are not entrusted with the responsibility with which the prophets (peace be upon them) are entrusted.

Comfort for the Messenger:
That is, if those who are of a miserable and deprived nature do not appreciate this great mercy, it is their own loss. Those who fear Allah will obtain a reminder from it, and they are the ones intended. Indeed, Allah revealed this blessing specifically for them. This comforts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): if the ungrateful do not appreciate it, do not be disheartened; after all, there are those who do appreciate it!

Warning to the opponents:
This is a threat and warning to the opponents: We know well that there are deniers among you, and We know who they are. The meaning is: those who are denying should remember that they are not hidden from Us, and they will face their end!

That is: 'And indeed, it will be a regret for the disbelievers.' Today they are denying it and are highly pleased with their actions, but very soon the Day is coming when it will become a source of deep regret for them, and they will beat their heads in misery, questioning why they invited their ruin by denying it!

That is, this is not a baseless claim, but an absolute certainty. The Day of Resurrection and recompense of which this Qur'an is warning will surely occur.
The point stated in the initial verses: (The Inevitable Reality. What is the Inevitable Reality? And what can make you know what is the Inevitable Reality?) is recalled at the conclusion of the Surah in a different style. Thus, the Surah concludes with the same theme with which it began. This style is adopted in most Surahs of the Qur'an, and serves as a clear proof of structural coherence in the Qur'an.

This comforts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): do not be disheartened by the way of those who deny, but be patient, wait, and glorify the name of your Great Lord. This glorification will be a source of patience, strength, and confidence for you. Allah is of great power and majesty; when the appointed time comes, He will show these rebels how His every word is fulfilled.
